coredata.dc:title: Exploring yellow emissive polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dots for sensing and removal of chromium ions in aqueous media
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Nawas Sherif, Z.
Anitha, K.
coredata.dc:description: The present study addresses the yellow emissive polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dots (YPC) for recognizing and removing pernicious chromium (Cr3+) ions from aqueous solutions. To sense and remove the Cr3+ ions in this case, YPC functions as both an adsorbent and a fluorescent sensor using the adsorption method and fluorescence quenching (Turn-Off), respectively. A successful optimization of the material YPCconcerning certain parameters, such as pH, time, YPC concentration, and metal ion concentration, has resulted in significant sensitivity and removal efficiency. The material has a high removal efficiency and a low detection limit of 0.04 ppm for Cr3+ions. Cr3+ ions are selected based on their fluorescence reaction to the material. The structural, optical, elemental composition and vibrational properties of YPC are examined, and the adsorption isotherm, kinetics and its mechanism of detection and elimination are examined. Lastly, the material is used with actual ambient water samples to assess its practicality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyethylenimine
Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensing
Adsorption
Chromium (Cr3+) ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Material synthesis and preparation methods for applications
Sample preparation for detection of Cr3+ ions
Sample preparation for the removal of Cr3+ions
Functional groups, surface charges and area of YPC
Concentration of Cr3+ and YPC optimization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by RASHTRIYA UCCHATAR SHIKSHA ABHIYAN (RUSA) (046/RUSA/MKU/2020\u20132021). The authors thank RUSA for providing financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PXRD pattern, (b) SEM image and (c) HRTEM image, (d) particle size distribution graph of YPC (using HRTEM).
(a) UV–Visible absorption spectrum of YPC (inset: Tauc plot of YPC), (b) Photoluminescence spectrum of YPC (inset: YPC under UV light (360 nm)) and (c) lifetime decay of YPC and YPC with Cr3+ ions.
(a) FTIR spectrum, Zeta potential graph of (b) YPC, (c) Cr3+ ion, (d) EDX spectrum of YPC, and (e) Excitation dependent emission.
(a) XPS full survey, and high resolution XPS of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s spectra of YPC.

YPC’s (a) selectivity analysis, (b) interference analysis, (c) pH optimization, (d) time optimization, (e) Sensitivity analysis, and (f) linear relation between I0/I and concentration of Cr3+ ions. Error bars represents the standard deviation of three repetitive analysis.
Adsorption efficiency of YPC based on (a) pH, (b) time, (c) initial concentration of Cr3+, (d) Freundlich adsorption isotherm, © Langmuir adsorption isotherm and (f) adsorption kinetic models.
(a) Stability of YPC, (b) Long-term stability of YPC, and (c) Reproducibility of YPC.
(a) Absorbance of metal ions, (b) Inner filter effect mechanism.
Limit of detection of present work compared with reported literature.
Comparison of YPC with reported work.

head.ce:title: Exploring yellow emissive polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dots for sensing and removal of chromium ions in aqueous media

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai 625021, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai 625021, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study addresses the yellow emissive polyethylenimine functionalized carbon dots (YPC) for recognizing and removing pernicious chromium (Cr3+) ions from aqueous solutions. To sense and remove the Cr3+ ions in this case, YPC functions as both an adsorbent and a fluorescent sensor using the adsorption method and fluorescence quenching (Turn-Off), respectively. A successful optimization of the material YPCconcerning certain parameters, such as pH, time, YPC concentration, and metal ion concentration, has resulted in significant sensitivity and removal efficiency. The material has a high removal efficiency and a low detection limit of 0.04 ppm for Cr3+ions. Cr3+ ions are selected based on their fluorescence reaction to the material. The structural, optical, elemental composition and vibrational properties of YPC are examined, and the adsorption isotherm, kinetics and its mechanism of detection and elimination are examined. Lastly, the material is used with actual ambient water samples to assess its practicality.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The contamination of water with heavy metal ions has become a major worldwide concern because of the risks it poses to both the environment and human health [1]. Because of their uncontrollably high rate of disposal into nature as a result of fast industrialization, Cr3+ions in particular have an impact on the environment [2]. Instances of skin irritation, ulcers, respiratory issues, weakened immune systems, renal and liver damage, and other issues can arise from concentrations of Cr3+ that are higher than the allowable limit [3]. To identify and eliminate Cr3+ions from the aqueous media, a material must be developed.

Numerousmaterials have been recently reported for the detection and removal of Cr3+ ions individually, such as anthracene and indole-based fluorescent probe with a limit of detection of 0.2 µM for Cr3+ ions, developed by Gunduz et al. [4] and coumarin based fluorescence probe reported by Xuan et al. has a limit of detection of 3.14 µM [5]. Further, Cr3+ is removed by using sodium polyacrylate hydrogel powder reported by Ouass et al. and bagasse and rice husk fiber-based amine magnetic biocomposite, reported by Iryanti et al. [6,7]. But this work focuses on the contemporaneous detection and removal of chromium ions by a unique material with high sensitivity and adsorption efficiency. Concerning the criteria, the carbon dot is employed to detect and eliminate chromium ions because of its special properties, which include outstanding photostability, reduced photo-bleaching, wide absorption and emission spectra, and a high surface-to-volume ratio [8]. It uses fluorescence sensing to detect metal ions instead of other optical detection techniques like absorption, reflection, and others since it has its advantages, which include rapid response rates [9,10] and unique attributes like the capacity to visually identifying target-heavy metal ions [11]. The adsorption approach is recommended for removal since it is economical, uncomplicated, residue-free, and reliable [12]. Here, polyethylenimine (PEI) is utilized to functionalize carbon dots to improve its performance since PEI has amine groups in it [8]. Thus, carbon dot is functionalized to achieve high efficiency towards the applications than the reported fluorescence probe and adsorbent materials, such as rhodamine 6 G detect Cr3+ ions with limit of detection of 1.6 × 10–8 M as reported by Mao et al. [13], pyrene compound has a limit of detection of 4 × 10–8 M for Cr3+ ion proposed by Li et al., [14] and adsorbent material such as meranti sawdust reported by Rafatullah et al., and lignin reported by Wu et.al. [15,16].

In this instance, solvothermal synthesis of polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots is employed, followed by its characterization and simultaneous detection and elimination of Cr3+ ions from the contaminated water [17]. Furthermore, the produced YPC nanomaterial's adsorption efficiency and fluorescence sensing are assessed using an analysis of its Limit of Detection (LOD), removal efficiency, adsorption isotherms,and adsorption kinetic study, in that order. Its viability is determined by evaluating it using real water samples.

Citric acid (CA), Urea, polyethylenimine (PEI) solution, phosphate standard for IC–buffer solution, aluminum chloride anhydrous, barium chloride dihydrate, cadmium acetate dihydrate, chromium (III) chloride hexahydrate, copper (II) chloride dihydrate, distilled water, lead nitrate, mercury (II) chloride, magnesium chloride hexahydrate, manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate, nickel (II) acetate, strontium chloride hexahydrate, tin (II) chloride, tungsten (VI) chloride, and zinc sulphate heptahydrate, were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Private Limited, Bangalore, India, in analytical grade, and used without additional purification.

The following instruments are used to characterize the prepared material: a PANalytical X-pert pro diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ=1.540 Å) operating at a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 30 mA was used to obtain the powder XRD patterns. The absorption spectra were recorded using the SHIMADZU UV-2450 spectrophotometer; the photoluminescence spectra were collected using the SHIMADZU RF-5301 spectrofluorophotometer; and the Fourier transform infrared data was recorded using the SHIMADZU FTIR-8400S. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN TEM, 200 kV) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (TESCAN VEGA3 SBH, 30 kV) were used to examine the morphology of the materials. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was used to confirm the elemental composition of the samples, and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha-KAN9954133) with an energy range of 100–4000 eV was used to perform X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy observations. The fluorophore's lifetime was measured using the JOBIN YUON Fluorolog-3–11 (Pulsed-Diode source; 340 nm), the Malvern Zetasizer MAL1052893 was used to measure the fluorophore's zeta potential, the surface area of the sample was analysed using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area analyser (AutosirbiQ, Quantachrome), and the SHIMADZU AA-6300 atomic absorption spectrophotometer was used to measure the concentration of metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material synthesis and preparation methods for applications

ce:section.ce:para: The solvothermal method was applied to synthesis YPC [18,19,20], with the precursors including citric acid, Urea, and polyethylenimine solution. Initially, citric acid (1.26 g)and urea (1.37 g) were dissolved in a 1:3 ratio along with a polyethylenimine (1 wt %) to the total amount of citric acid and urea,were dissolved in a 24 ml of mixture solution containing distilled water and dimethylformamide in 1:5 ratio and stirred for 20 min. After achieving homogeneity, the mixture was autoclaved and maintained at 160 °C for four hours. The material was then preserved for later use after being filtered via a 0.22 µm filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation for detection of Cr3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: At first, stock solutions of the metal ions mentioned in the chemical section were made with a concentration of 1 mM. To conduct a selectivity analysis, 300 µl of YPC was diluted with 500 µl of buffer solution (10 mM) and mixed with 30 µl of each metal ion. Further, to examine the interference analysis, several metal ions of the same concentration, along with the selected target analyte (Cr3+) were introduced to YPC. The sensitivity will then be examined by adding different Cr3+ ion concentrations (0.5 µM to 35 µM) to prepared YPC [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation for the removal of Cr3+ions
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the solution (YPC with metal ion) was adjusted by sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid to perform the adsorption experiment. Then, various amounts of metal ions were added to YPC using the procedure for sensitivity to investigate the adsorption efficiency of YPC [22].

The effectiveness of the prepared YPC nanomaterial for the detection and removal of Cr3+ ions has been evaluated using tap, river, and lake water [21]. We gathered water from lakes, rivers, and taps in the local vicinity. A 0.22 µm filter was used to filter the real water samples in order to remove the suspended particles. The samples were then treated with 0.3 µM and 0.8 µM of Cr3+ ions for detection, and 0.5 mM and 1 mM of Cr3+ ions for removal. After that, real water samples were added to the YPC solution using the method (sample preparation for sensitivity) that was previously explained.

The diffraction pattern of powder X-rays is recorded between 10° and 80° of diffraction angle to know the structure and crystallinity of YPC. There is a broad, less-intense diffraction peak at an angle of 23° that corresponds to the (002) plane (Fig. 1 (a)). Additionally, the amorphous nature of the produced material with smaller particle sizes is shown by the broad diffraction peak [23]. Then YPC’s morphology is analysed using SEM and HRTEM analysis. Thus, the produced material has a spherical morphology, as detected by scanning electron microscopy (Fig. 1(b)). This observation is further supported by HRTEM as shown in Fig. 1(c), which shows an average particle size of around 6 nm (Fig. 1(d)) [24].

In optical analysis, the absorption of YPC is analysed by a spectrophotometer, further, a spectrofluorophotometer is utilized to investigate its emission, lifetime and quantum yield. According to Fig. 2 (a), YPC has an absorption peak at 360 nm due to C = O, n-π* transition. The corresponding band gap energy of YPC is found to be 2.9 eV as indicated in Fig. 2(a)-inset, which is obtained by applying the Tauc’s plot followed by absorption data [25,26]. In other words, when YPC is excited at 500 nm, it exhibits a high emission at 560 nm (Fig. 2(b)), and when it is exposed to 360 nm of UV light, it emits a yellow colour(inset: Fig. 2(b)). It is determined that the average lifetime decay of YPC is 3.36 ns, and the average lifetime of YPC with Cr3+is reduced to 2.10 ns due to its fluorescence quenching response with Cr3+ions (Fig. 2(c)) [27]. Then the quantum yield of YPC is determined as 20.22 % [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional groups, surface charges and area of YPC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a) represents the FTIR analysis of YPC,recorded from 400 cm-1 to 4000 cm-1region to determine the functional groups of YPC. OH stretching is indicated by the broad and strong peak at 3407 cm-1 [26]. The alkyl group's carbon and hydrogen (CH) bond's stretching vibration caused the peaks at 2933 cm-1 and 2870 cm-1 [24]. The presence of NH bond stretching and bending vibration is confirmed by peaks at 1649 cm-1 [29], while CH bending vibration is the cause of the peak at 1494 cm-1 [24]. CO-C bending vibrations are associated with the peaks at 1388 cm-1 and 1254 cm-1 [26,30]. Peaks at 1061 cm-1 correlate to CO stretching, and peak observations at 1098 cm-1 demonstrate CH bending vibration [28,31]. The stretching vibration of CN bond is attributed to the peak that emerged at 658 cm-1 [23]. The presence of the NH bond indicates the interaction between polyethylenimine and carbon dot, which enhances the properties of the carbon dots and their solubility [29,31]. More significantly, YPC's enormous oxygen groups imply the biocompatibility of carbon dots [32]. Further, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and zeta potential characterizations are performed to determine the sample's surface area and surface charges, respectively. As a result, YPC has a large surface area of about 15.82 m2/g and it is strongly anionic in nature, with a zeta potential of 0.3 mV (Fig. 3(b)).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) study reveals the surface elemental composition of YPC.As a result, the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen on the surface of YPC is shown by the three major peaks in the XPS spectra (Fig. 4 (a)) at 285.51 eV, 399.78 eV, and 531.85 eV, which correspond to the C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [17,24,26,31]. In the high-resolution spectrum, the C1s band may be deconvoluted into four peaks, which correspond to C = C, CC, CO, and C = O, at 284.35 eV, 285.09 eV, 285.84 eV, and 287.46 eV, respectively (Fig. 4(b)). Two deconvoluted peaks of N1s spectrum are at 399.61 eV and 399.97 eV(Fig. 4(c)), represents the presence of NC and NH groups, respectively. At 531.11 eV, 531.85 eV, and 532.95 eV, the O1s exhibit three peaks, respectively, indicating the presence of C = O, OH, and O = C O (Fig. 4(d)).

In addition, the elemental composition of YPC is studied using energy dispersive X-ray analysis. As demonstrated in Fig. 3(d) [33], EDX analysis verifies the existence of three elements in the synthesized YPC: carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, together with the weight proportion.

Carbon dot itself a fluorophore, which has its unique fluorescence characteristics. Here, it exhibits a distinct, intense yellow emission at 560 nm when excited at 500 nm, while varying excitation wavelengths.Thus, surface state emission of the carbon dots is revealed in Fig. 3(e), which shows that the emission depends on the excitation wavelength.The presence of surface functional groups, such as CN, CO, etc., which cause the surface state emission,is confirmed by both FTIR and XPS studies [34]. Meanwhile, numerous oxygen groups on the surface cause the band gap to be narrow and the emission to be red-shifted. HRTEM analysis confirms the lower dimension of YPC's particle size, which is approximately 6 nm. The powder X-ray diffraction pattern’s broad diffraction peak also serves as an indirect representation of the particle size. In terms of the quantum confinement effect, the particle size and emission colour are correlated with the decreased band gap energy [34].

YPC's low dimension reveals its high surface-to-volume ratio, indicating that it has a large surface area and a greater number of adsorption active sites to adsorb heavy metal ions [35]. BET study, which shows that YPC has a larger surface area of roughly 15.82 m2/g, validates this statement. Additionally, YPC's stronger anionic nature permits it to electrostatically bind the target analyte [35]. The presence of amine groups (NH) at 1649 cm-1with binding energy of 419.33 eV, induces a complex with metal ions, which facilitates the adsorption of YPC [36,37].

In the selectivity process, several metal ions such as Aluminium (Al3+), Barium (Ba2+), Cadmium (Cd2+), Chromium (Cr3+), Copper (Cu2+), Mercury (Hg2+), Magnesium (Mg2+), Manganese (Mn2+), Nickel (Ni2+), Lead (Pb2+), Tin (Sn2+), Strontium (Sr2+), Tungsten (W6+), and Zinc (Zn2+) have been introduced to the material YPC to select a particular metal ion based on the metal ion’s fluorescence emission response. All metal ions display higher fluorescence in response to the synthesized YPC, but Cr3+ exhibits diminished fluorescence as shown in Fig. 5 (a). As a result ofthe quenching response of Cr3+ ("turn-off" mechanism) towards YPC, it is selected as a target analyte.

The selectivity of Cr3+ ions can be actively supported by examining the interference of other metal ions into the prepared YPC in addition to the Cr3+ ions. As seen in Fig. 5(b), YPC's fluorescence quenches even in the presence of other metal ions, with Cr3+ ions. This shows that the turn-off mechanism can be used to detect Cr3+ ions even in the presence of other metal ions.

The effect of pH can either increase or decrease YPC's ability to sense the target Cr3+ion. In this case, the YPC dosage was 4 mg/l and the Cr3+ ion concentration was 1 µM. The pH of the YPC solution was then adjusted between pH 3 and pH 9.Throughout the study, the optimal pH of 7 has been used. Because the enhanced fluorescence emission is observed at pH 7 in contrast to other acidic and basic regions (Fig. 5(c)), this fluorescence behaviour is caused by the electron-transferring effects of amine groups on the surface of YPC [34].

Moreover, time has been optimised by recording the fluorescence spectrum at different times in order to attain fluorescence sensing efficiency. For around half an hour, the spectra are recorded every five minutes, but the fluorescence intensity remains constant (Fig. 5(d)). As a result, the prepared carbon dot is found to have low photo-bleaching properties and to remain vibrant for at least 30 min. This time frame is sufficient to set up preliminary arrangements to conduct further investigation.

In general, the carbon dot concentration is rarely changed to improve sensing efficiency, because the intensity of the fluorescence decreases as the YPC concentration rises. As a result, a very low concentration of YPC is used to improve efficiency, and this efficiency is further achieved by adjusting other reported factors. The fluorescence intensity is decreased for the optimized concentration of YPC, and it is only detectable by instrumental analysis rather than by visual means.This decrease in fluorescence intensity is caused by self-absorption of the irradiation source, which arises from a decrease in inter-particle spacing and promotes stronger particle interaction while increasing the concentration of carbon dots [34].

Variation in the concentration of metal ions is directly related to the sensing ability of YPC. Hence, Cr3+ ions were added to the YPC solution at different concentrations ranging from 0.5 µM to 35 µM, to examine the impact of concentration. In this experiment, the fluorescence of YPC steadily reduced as the concentration increased (Fig. 5(e)), of which were explained by the fluorescence turn-off process.

By making use of above mentioned sensitivity process, that is, the variation of metal ions concentration process, the limit of detection is calculated. It correlates the concentration of Cr3+ ions with the fluorescence quenching (I0/I), which, before and after the Cr3+ ion is incorporated, is represented by the fluorescence intensities I0 and I [18]. According to Fig. 5(f) there is a projected linear calibration curve between 0.5 µM and 8 µM. A lower detection limit of 0.04 ppm was established, with a linear regression value R2 is equivalent to 0.995. using LOD = 3σ/s, where ‘σ’ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and s represents the slope of the calibration curve [38]. Finally, the present study is compared with previously reported data in Table 1 .

Determination of adsorption efficiency to evaluate YPC’s tendency to adsorb Cr3+ ions to it is significant. Hence, following equation ((C0
C1)/C0)) x 100 is used to ascertain the adsorption efficiency of YPC on adsorbing Cr3+ ionsin upcoming batch experiments such as pH, time, concentration of YPC and concentration of metal ion optimizations, and the representation ((C0
C1) x (V/W)) (mg/g) is used to calculate the adsorption capacity. Where, W is the adsorbent mass in grams, C0 and C1 are the known initial and final concentrations of metal ions, and V is the working solution volume in litres [33,39].

Accordingly, as shown in Fig. 6 (a), adsorption of hydrogen ions in acidic regions, and precipitation of metal ions in basic regions, lead to low adsorption efficiency because of the non-interaction between the metal ions and YPC. Hence, neutral pH 7 is ascertained as an optimum pH condition to carry out the experiment because it shows a high adsorption efficiency of about 85 % [40,41].

The YPC's reaction time with the Cr3+ ion is measured by tracking its adsorption for almost half an hour (Fig. 6(b)). But by five minutes, the adsorption efficiency reaches a maximum of over 80 %, and it increases as the duration increases. Consequently, the reaction can reach high adsorption efficiency in just five minutes. This short time frame demonstrates how quickly YPC reacts with Cr3+ ions that are being adsorbed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration of Cr3+ and YPC optimization
ce:section.ce:para: In adsorption tests, concentrations of Cr3+ ions were changed from (1 µM to 11 µM), to examine the impact of concentration. Its removal effectiveness consequently declines from 79.6 % to 43.4 % with the reduction of adsorption active sites. However, as Fig. 6(c) illustrates, adsorption capacity rises from 41.4 mg/g to 283.2 mg/g as Cr3+ion concentration increases due to adsorption of Cr3+ ions on the YPC by occupying higher number of adsorption active sites. Once the Cr3+ ion concentration is optimized, the YPC concentration responds in reverse manner. The efficiency can be raised by increasing the adsorption activation site in response to a higher YPC concentration. Adsorption efficiency can therefore be raised by either raising the concentration of YPC or decreasing the concentration of Cr3+ions. The effectiveness of YPC with other known adsorbents is then shown in Table 2 .

Freundlich adsorption isotherm and Langmuir adsorption isotherm models are studied to ascertain the adsorption capacity of YPC [42]. The Freundlich model is expressed as log qe = log KF + 1/n log Ce, and the Langmuir model can be represented as 1/qe = (1/KLQm.1/Ce + 1/Qm). Where Qm is the maximum concentration of Cr3+ ions adsorbed on YPC, KL stands for Langmuir constant, KF for the Freundlich constant, and 1/n is the adsorption intensity. Moreover, Ce is the equilibrium concentration of adsorbate (Cr3+ ions) in the solution, expressed in mg/L.

Table 3 presents the findings of the adsorption parameter evaluations by the Langmuir and Freundlich models based on experimental data (adsorption experiments). The Cr3+ ion adsorption on YPC in this instance is multilayer adsorption, as evidenced by a higher linear regression coefficient for the Freundlich adsorption isotherm (Fig. 6(d)) than the Langmuir isotherm (Fig. 6(e)). Physical adsorption results from the elimination of Cr3+ ions from the water [43,44].

Here, the adsorption rate was evaluated by employing the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics models. ln (Qe-Qt) = lnQe-K1t and t/Qt = (1/Qe)t + 1/K2Qe 2 are the representations of pseudo-first-order kinetics and pseudo-second-order kinetics, respectively [40], where Qe represents the concentrations of adsorbate (mg/g) on the adsorbent at equilibrium and Qt denotes the concentrations of adsorbate (mg/g) on the adsorbent at time t. K1 is the first-order rate constant per minute, and K2 for the second-order rate constant per minute. As seen in Fig. 6(f), pseudo-second-order kinetics thus suits the experimental data (Table 4 ) better than pseudo-first-order kinetics. It displays YPC's chemisorption behaviour on Cr3+ ion adsorption [40].

Short-term and long-term duration withstand of YPC's fluorescence intensity are used to analyse the stability of the prepared YPC, maintained with a neutral pH and at room temperature. Then, the fluorescence behaviour is recorded for approximately 150 min in the short-term duration analysis, and the fluorescence intensity is recorded for approximately three months in the long-term duration analysis by dispersing a particular concentration of YPC in water. Whether the analysis is short-term or long-term, both durations show that the prepared YPC has good stability. Fig. 7 (a,b) shows that its behaviour has not changed significantly, indicating the material's long-term stability. As a result, mass synthesis can be performed at once and used for various analyses for a long period [45].

Future preparation and commercialisation of the material YPC depend on its reproducibility [46]. To determine the repeatability of YPC, it is synthesized five times using the previously described procedure, and the fluorescence effect is recorded.Typically prepared, YPC exhibits the same activity throughout a number of repetitions by adhering to a stable concentration and quantity of precursors and procedures. As a result, YPC can be efficiently prepared for various other applications in the future.Fig. 7(c) shows the recorded fluorescence response, which is anticipated at the same wavelength and correlates with the emission colour and particle size.

Fluorescence quenching results from the addition of Cr3+ ions; hence, static and dynamic quenching techniques are possible. In dynamic quenching, the interaction takes place at an excited state, in contrast to static quenching, where the fluorophore and quencher interact in the ground state [47]. These mechanisms can be anticipated by taking into account the sample's absorbance, emission, lifetime decay, static quenching constant, and dynamic quenching constant. On analysing the lifetime study of the fluorophore, the lifetime of YPC is contrasted with its lifetime when a quencher is present. A static quenching mechanism would result when both lifetimes remain constant, but Fig. 2(c) shows a fall in lifetime from 3.36 ns to 2.10 ns, indicating a dynamic quenching mechanism [48]. Following that, the dynamic quenching constant (KD) and static quenching constant (KS) are determined by applying Stern-Volmer (S-V) relations, such as I0/I = 1+(KD+KS) [Q] + (KDKS) [Q]2 and τ/τ0 = 1 + KD [Q] [49]. In this case, Q represents the metal ion concentration, and τ and τ0 for the material's lifetime decay in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively. As a result, the KS and KD are calculated as 45 × 103 and 17 × 103, respectively. This represents that the quenching occurs based on the static quenching mechanism as a result of a high static quenching constant value [50]. Thus, static quenching occurs by forming a ground-state complex between YPC and Cr3+ ions. This happens when there is an interaction between the Cr3+ ions and functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, such as hydroxyl groups, amine groups and carboxylic groups, resulting in chelation and thereby forming a coordination complex between carbon dots and the metal ions [51,52,53].

The Inner Filter Effect (IFE) is examined using absorbance and fluorescence emission spectra in addition to static and dynamic quenching mechanisms. When the absorbance of different metal ions that are all employed in the selectivity process is examined, it is found that Cr3+ ions have a distinct absorbance peak that appears between 410 and 577 nm (Fig. 8 (a)). The fact that these peaks overlap with the YPC's emission and absorbance peaks is significant (Fig. 8(b)). This overlap indicates that the incident and emission photons of YPC are absorbed by the Cr3+ ions. As a result, the absorption of these photons leads to a decrease in the fluorescence emission of YPC. Thus, the inner filter effect influences the quenching mechanism [54,55,56]. After conducting a thorough analysis, it has been determined that the quenching of fluorescence, which aids in identifying Cr3+ ions, is caused by both dynamic quenching and inner filter effects.

In the adsorption mechanism, catalytic Cr3+ ions and anionic carbon dots interact electrostatically. Furthermore, cations that are highly attracted to the anionic YPC cause the fluorescence of the polyethyleneimine carbon dot to be significantly quenched. In contrast, YPC has a somewhat anionic nature with a value of around 0.3 mV, and Cr3+ ions have a cationic nature with a value of 17 mV according to Zeta potential (Fig. 3(b,c)). The electrostatic contact causes YPC to efficiently adsorb Cr3+ ions [57]. According to the isotherm and kinetic analysis, the experimental data is better fitted by the Freundlich adsorption isotherm model and the pseudo-second order kinetic model, respectively. Both results contradict that adsorption occurs based on physisorption and chemisorption. In this case, electrostatic contact is classified as both chemical and physical adsorption [58]. The electrostatic mechanism that promotes the adsorption process is then associated with the discussion of static quenching of fluorescence.

Table 5 illustrates the experimental results that indicate how effectively the YPC nanomaterial recognizes and eliminates Cr3+ ions from actual samples. Thus, the fluorescence quenching percentage rises, and the adsorption efficiency falls with increasing Cr3+ ion concentration.

The YPC nanomaterial was synthesized, characterized, and employed in this investigation to successfully identify and remove the Cr3+ ions from the aqueous medium. With a particle size of roughly 6 nm, amorphous YPC has a quasi-spherical morphology. YPC has distinct optical properties, including yellow emission at a wavelength of 560 nm and a lifetime of roughly 3.36 ns. Its functional groups and elemental composition is revealed by FTIR, XPS, and EDX analysis. Then, the surface charge of YPC is found to be 15.82 m2/g.

In applications, YPC possesses a lower limit of detection of 0.04 ppm, which is less than the World Health Organisation’s (WHO) permissible limit (0.05 ppm) [9] and has an adsorption efficiency of about 99 %. Further isotherm and kinetic studies reveal that the Freundlich adsorption isotherm and the pseudo-second-order kinetics fit better with the experimental results. Additionally, YPC has good stability and reproducibility. Finally, practicability analysis shows its effectiveness in detecting and removing Cr3+ ions from real environmental water samples.
Z. Nawas Sherif: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. K. Anitha: Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: K. Anitha reports financial support was provided by Rashtriya Ucchatar Shiksha Abhiyan. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially supported by RASHTRIYA UCCHATAR SHIKSHA ABHIYAN (RUSA) (046/RUSA/MKU/2020–2021). The authors thank RUSA for providing financial support.
